svg.text: Evolution of small-db's transaction model
Each stage solves the previous stage's blocker. Read top to bottom.
Stage 0 — Distributed KV without concurrency control
Writes hit storage immediately at wall-clock ts. No isolation, no MVCC.
Anomalies: lost updates, torn reads, read-write contention everywhere.
Problem: readers must wait for writers; concurrent
writes overwrite each other, losing effects.
Insight: readers don't need the freshest value —
a coherent past snapshot is enough. Let writes create new versions.
Stage 1 — MVCC: multiple versions per row, snapshot reads
Each write produces a new version tagged with ts. Reads filter by snapshot_ts.
✓ Readers never block writers and see consistent point-in-time data (per row).
Problem: concurrent writers to the same row both read
the pre-image, both write — one's effect is silently lost.
Insight: writers to the same row must serialize so the
second one sees the first's commit before computing.
Stage 2 — Per-row exclusive lock + read-latest under the lock
A writer locks the row, reads the latest committed value, computes, writes, releases.
✓ Concurrent writers can't lose updates within a single statement.
Problem: a transfer is BEGIN/UPDATE/UPDATE/COMMIT —
two writes at two different ts. A reader can land between them.
Insight: all writes of one transaction must share a single
commit_ts so a snapshot either sees them all or none.
Stage 3 — Multi-statement transactions with one shared commit_ts
BEGIN buffers writes; COMMIT picks one ts and dispatches every buffered write at that ts.
✓ A snapshot at any ts sees every row of one transaction together (or none).
Problem (shadowed writes): two concurrent commits from
different coordinators arrive at the same row out of order. The
chronologically-later writer's commit_ts can be smaller. Its
write is lex-shadowed and silently dropped from view.
Insight: observing a value should advance the observer's
timestamp past it. The shadowed write happened only because
T1 didn't raise its commit_ts after seeing T2's value during
read-latest.
Stage 4 — A tension we cannot escape with per-row mechanisms
Need (a): commit_ts must exceed every prior version_ts on every row this txn touches (closes shadowing)
Need (b): all of one txn's writes must land at the same commit_ts (keeps multi-row atomicity)
Naive options that don't compose:
• Bump version_ts per row independently → satisfies (a) but breaks (b).
• Pre-bump commit_ts at COMMIT, then dispatch — works, but changing it late forces
rewriting all of the txn's writes (O(N) at commit time, on the hot path).
Tension: we want one commit_ts per txn that may
be raised at commit time, but rewriting N row entries
at commit is too expensive on the hot path.
Insight: decouple "the value sitting on this row" from
"is this committed, and at what ts." Move the second piece
into one shared place that the rows point to.
Stage 5 — Intents + txn record (the destination)
Each write lays down an intent on the row: {value, txn_id}. The intent is the row's MVCC entry,
just with a forwarding pointer instead of a final ts.
A separate txn record, keyed by txn_id, holds {status, commit_ts}. This is the single source of
truth for "is the txn committed, and at what ts."
COMMIT = one update of the txn record (O(1)). All N intents flip to visible together. Push = the
same one update. Lazy resolution rewrites intents to canonical version entries in the background.
✓ Single commit_ts per txn (atomicity). ✓ Push past existing versions without rewriting any rows.
✓ Conflict signal lasts from first write to commit (the row lock released at end-of-statement could not).
Each blue stage is a model we actually adopted. Each red callout is the failure that drove us forward.
Each yellow callout is the principle that picked the next design. Green at the bottom is where the chain lands.
